text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure by removing the gastroesophageal fat pad using an energy device to cauterize the small blood vessels at the gastroesophageal junction. After removing the fat pad, the surgeon measures the lower stomach with a grasper to identify the pylorus and the greater curve. The assistant elevates the stomach as the surgeon transects the short gastric vessels, joining the upper part of the stomach. A bougie sizing tube is passed into the stomach, and the surgeon uses a stapling device to create a sleeve alongside the fifty French bougie. The sleeve gastrectomy portion is completed, followed by oversewing the staple line in an inverting fashion to prevent leaks, ensuring no staples are visible on the outside of the stomach.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to create a window under and above the duodenum, carefully transecting small blood vessels to form a clear tunnel behind the duodenum. A blunt device is passed posterior to the duodenum, followed by the use of a stapling device to transect the duodenum, completing this portion of the duodenal switch. The surgeon measures the bowel using a ruler, identifying the ligament of Treitz and marking the bowel at one hundred centimeters with clips for the lower anastomosis. The bowel is further measured to two hundred and fifty centimeters for the alimentary limb, which is rotated into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon rotates the loop of small bowel at the 250-centimeter mark towards the upper abdomen and begins sewing it to the duodenal staple line using a two-layered anastomosis. The posterior layer is created with an endo-stitch device and absorbable suture in a running fashion across the staple line and ileum. Openings are made in the duodenum and ileum using electrocautery, ensuring they are wide to prevent strictures. The inner layer is sewn with a vial suture, followed by the anterior inner layer, carefully avoiding the back wall to maintain the opening.

The surgeon completes the anterior inner layer of the anastomosis by tying it to the posterior inner layer, followed by the final anterior outer layer using an inverting suture technique in a running fashion. The biliopancreatic limb is then transected with a stapling device, disconnecting bile and pancreatic enzymes from this limb. An endoscopy is performed to test the airtightness of the gastric sleeve and duodenal ileostomy, showing no air leakage.

The surgeon locates the clips at the 100-centimeter mark on the small bowel, indicating the site for reattaching the biliopancreatic limb. An opening is created in the biliopancreatic limb, and it is attached to the alimentary limb using a double staple technique. The stapler is positioned to create an opening between the two limbs, ensuring a wide anastomosis without stricture. The bowel is rotated to facilitate this, and the stapler is fired in the opposite direction to complete the anastomosis, followed by closing the common opening.

The surgeon completes the ilioiliostomy and closes the mesenteric defect using a permanent suture to prevent herniation of the small bowel. A fibrin sealant is applied to seal the duodenal iliostomy. The gastric specimen is removed through a fifteen millimeter port with a wound protector, and the liver retractor is taken out, concluding the laparoscopic duodenal switch procedure.
